chunks.text: The birth of cinema was a miraculous invention at the end of the nineteenth century.
Since the Lumiere brothers held a public screening in Paris in 1895
film has become one of humanity's arts.
Early films were silent.
The story had to be conveyed through the actor's facial expression and gesture alone.
The greatest star of that era was Charlie Chaplin.
Chaplin was born in 1889 to a poor family in London.
His father died young from alcoholism, and his mother suffered mental illness.
At seven, Chaplin was sent to an orphanage.
His childhood was a battle against awful poverty.
He kept alive on thick soup and old bread.
But that experience gave depth to his later work.
At twenty - five
Chaplin crossed to Hollywood and invented the character of " the Tramp.
" A bowler hat, a cane, a tiny mustache, shoes too big.
This figure was loved around the world.
His films were comedies, yet they sharply depicted the contradictions of society.
" Modern Times " satirized the alienation of industrial society
" The Great Dictator " satirized Nazi Germany.
In the 1950 s, however, when the Red Scare began in the United States
Chaplin was suspected of being a communist sympathizer.
He left the US and went into exile in Switzerland.
In his later years, Chaplin's honor was restored.
At the 1972 Academy Awards
the entire audience stood up and gave him a standing ovation.
The twelve - minute applause was the longest in Oscar history.
Chaplin's films do not fade even today.
" Life is a tragedy when seen up close, but a comedy from a distance.
" His words touch the truth of the human condition.
